_purpose: Local economy timeline for the OH Southeast Appalachian regional cluster (Washington, Athens, Meigs, Gallia, Lawrence, Scioto, Jackson, Vinton counties). Covers 1788-1960 across six economic eras — from the Ohio Company settlement through Appalachian poverty and out-migration. The economy of southeastern Ohio was a series of boom-bust cycles — salt, iron, coal, oil — layered on a subsistence farming base that could never support the population the booms attracted.
display_name: OH Southeast Appalachian — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.era_name: Frontier Settlement and the Northwest Ordinance
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Subsistence farming on river bottoms and creek valleys; salt production; fur trade; Ohio Company organized settlement at Marietta; Virginia and Kentucky migration fills the hill country
economic_eras.description: The settlement of southeastern Ohio began with the Ohio Company of Associates at Marietta (1788) — the first organized American settlement under the Northwest Ordinance, which prohibited slavery north of the Ohio River. The Ohio Company settlers were New England veterans who brought institutional culture: courts, schools, and churches from the first year. But the Ohio Company controlled only the river bottoms. Within a decade, the hill country behind the rivers was being settled by migrants from Virginia and Kentucky who crossed the Ohio River — many of them small farmers and landless laborers fleeing the economic competition of slave labor. These two streams — organized New England settlement on the river and Southern backcountry migration into the hills — created the cultural and economic divide that defined the region. The economy was pure subsistence: corn, potatoes, hogs on mast, and hunting. Salt production at the Scioto Salt Springs (Jackson/Scioto county border) and smaller springs throughout the region was the first significant industry — salt was the essential frontier commodity.

county_specifics.note: Ohio Company settlement at Marietta (1788). Campus Martius fortification. The most organized and documented of all Ohio frontier settlements. New England institutional culture from the first year.
Settled from Marietta outward. Ohio University chartered 1804, opened 1808 — the first university in the Northwest Territory. The university brought institutional presence to remote hill country.
Not yet a separate county (formed 1819). Ohio River settlements along the Great Bend. Salt springs along Leading Creek.

Gallipolis settled by defrauded French émigrés (1790). Most French settlers were urban artisans with no frontier skills. Many died or left. Virginia and Kentucky migrants replaced them within a generation.
Not yet a separate county (formed 1815). Ohio River settlements. Iron ore deposits not yet exploited.

Formed 1803. The Scioto Salt Springs were the most valuable resource in the region — the state reserved the salt lands and leased them to operators. Portsmouth established at the Ohio-Scioto confluence.
Not yet a separate county (formed 1816). Scioto Salt Springs on the border. First settlers were Virginia and Kentucky migrants.
Not yet a county (formed 1850). Unsettled wilderness — the last area to be populated in the region.

key_infrastructure.name: Campus Martius, Marietta
key_infrastructure.impact: Fortified settlement — the first organized American community in the Northwest Territory. Set the institutional standard for the region.

key_infrastructure.name: Scioto Salt Springs
key_infrastructure.impact: Ohio's most productive salt source. State-owned and leased to operators. Drew settlers and workers to the Scioto-Jackson county area.

key_infrastructure.name: Gallipolis settlement
key_infrastructure.impact: French settlers defrauded by the Scioto Company. Congress eventually provided a compensating French Grant. Cultural impact faded within a generation.

major_employers.name: Self-employment: subsistence farming, hunting
major_employers.employment_scale: Universal — there were no employers in the modern sense outside the salt works.

major_employers.name: Scioto Salt Springs operators
major_employers.employment_scale: Dozens of workers per operation. Salt production was labor-intensive — cutting wood, hauling brine, tending fires.

wage_benchmarks.source: Jakle, John A. 'Salt and the Initial Settlement of the Ohio Valley.' Ph.D. diss., Indiana University, 1967.
Cayton, Andrew R. L. The Frontier Republic. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1986.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived at the Ohio River in {year}, the choice was the organized settlement at Marietta — with its New England rules and its courthouse and its library — or the open hill country upstream, where a family could claim land without permission and live by what they grew, shot, and salted.
economic_eras.source: Hildreth, Samuel P. Pioneer History. Cincinnati: H. W. Derby, 1848. Cayton, Andrew R. L. The Frontier Republic: Ideology and Politics in the Ohio Country, 1780-1825. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1986. Jakle, 'Salt and the Initial Settlement of the Ohio Valley' (1967).

economic_eras.era_name: Iron, Salt, and the Steamboat Economy
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Hanging Rock charcoal iron furnaces; salt production; Ohio River steamboat commerce; subsistence farming on hill country; Ohio & Erie Canal reaches Portsmouth (1832)
economic_eras.description: The antebellum era brought the first industrial economy to southeastern Ohio: the Hanging Rock Iron Region. Between the 1820s and the Civil War, over seventy charcoal blast furnaces operated in Lawrence, Scioto, Jackson, and Vinton counties, producing pig iron prized for its quality. The industry consumed forest at an extraordinary rate — one acre of hardwood per furnace per day — and employed hundreds of workers: founders, colliers, ore miners, teamsters, and woodcutters. The ironmasters were the economic elite, building mansions in Ironton and operating self-contained furnace communities. Meanwhile, salt production continued at the Scioto Salt Springs and smaller operations throughout the region. The Ohio & Erie Canal reached Portsmouth in 1832, connecting the region to interior Ohio and transforming Portsmouth into a commercial hub. The Ohio River remained the primary transportation artery, and the steamboat economy peaked in the 1850s — dozens of boats per day passed through the region. But away from the river and the furnaces, the hill-country economy remained pure subsistence: small farms on steep slopes producing corn, potatoes, and hogs for household consumption.

county_specifics.note: Marietta remained the institutional center but fell behind Portsmouth and Ironton economically. No iron deposits. River commerce and modest agriculture.
Ohio University grew slowly. Salt and some iron furnace activity. Coal deposits identified but not yet commercially exploited.
Ohio River commerce. Salt production along Leading Creek. Coal mining began on a small scale.
Welsh settlement along Raccoon Creek began in the 1840s. Mixed farming and small-scale industry. French cultural presence had faded.
Epicenter of the Hanging Rock Iron Region. Ironton founded 1849 as an iron-shipping port. Dozens of furnaces operated in the county. The ironmasters were the wealthiest individuals in the region.
Ohio & Erie Canal terminus at Portsmouth (1832). Salt production. Iron furnaces. Commercial hub for the region.
Scioto Salt Springs. Iron furnaces in the eastern portion. Welsh settlement. Mixed economy of salt, iron, and farming.
County formed 1850. Iron furnaces. Most rural county in the region — settled by families too poor to buy river-bottom land.

key_infrastructure.name: Hanging Rock Iron Region furnaces
key_infrastructure.impact: Over seventy charcoal blast furnaces produced pig iron shipped by river to Cincinnati and Louisville. Each furnace was a self-contained community: furnace stack, charcoal kilns, ore banks, company store, worker housing. The industry consumed forest, employed hundreds, and made the ironmasters rich.

key_infrastructure.name: Ohio & Erie Canal terminus, Portsmouth
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Portsmouth to Chillicothe, Columbus, and Cleveland by water. Transformed Portsmouth from a river landing into a commercial junction. Goods could now move from the Ohio River to Lake Erie.

key_infrastructure.name: Steamboat traffic on the Ohio River
key_infrastructure.impact: Peak steamboat era. Dozens of boats per day passed through the region. River towns prospered; interior communities remained isolated.

major_employers.name: Hanging Rock iron furnaces (multiple operators)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands across the region. A single furnace employed 50-100 workers directly plus dozens of colliers and ore miners.

major_employers.name: Scioto Salt Springs and satellite salt works
Subsistence farming
major_employers.employment_scale: Universal in the hill country. 80%+ of families farmed.

wage_benchmarks.source: Stout, Wilber, and R. A. Schoenlaub. The Iron Ore Resources of Ohio. Columbus: Ohio Geological Survey, 1945.
Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945).
Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945).
Cayton, The Frontier Republic (1986).

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The Hanging Rock furnaces turned forest and ore into iron — and the iron went down the river to build the railroads and the bridges of a country that was expanding faster than its forests could feed the furnaces. {ancestor_name}'s wages at the furnace were better than anything the hill farm could produce, but the furnace consumed the forest as fast as the work consumed the workers.
economic_eras.source: Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945). Scheiber, Ohio Canal Era (1969). Jakle, 'Salt and the Initial Settlement of the Ohio Valley' (1967).

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Iron production for the Union war effort; river as military corridor; Morgan's Raid; Home Guard; subsistence farming under wartime conditions
economic_eras.description: Southeastern Ohio was a Union border region — the Ohio River separated free Ohio from slave-state Virginia (and after 1863, West Virginia). The region sent thousands of men to the Union army and experienced the war at close range. John Hunt Morgan's Confederate cavalry raid crossed the Ohio into Meigs and Washington counties in July 1863 — the northernmost Confederate penetration of the war — terrifying the civilian population. The Hanging Rock iron furnaces operated at capacity producing pig iron for the Union war effort, and furnace workers were sometimes exempted from military service because of the strategic importance of iron production. The Underground Railroad had operated through the region for decades, with crossing points at Ironton, Gallipolis, and Marietta. During the war, the flow of freedom seekers continued and accelerated.

county_specifics.note: Morgan's Raid crossed the Ohio River near Portland (Buffington Island area) in July 1863. The Battle of Buffington Island was fought in Meigs County just upstream. Marietta mobilized Home Guard units.
Ohio University students and faculty enlisted. Coal mining began to supply wartime demand.

Battle of Buffington Island (July 19, 1863) — Morgan's raiders were intercepted as they tried to recross the Ohio. The battle was fought along the river in Meigs County. Some raiders were captured; others continued north.
Ohio River crossing point for freedom seekers from Virginia. Home Guard mobilized against Morgan's Raid threat.
Iron furnaces operated at wartime capacity. Strategic importance of Hanging Rock iron kept some workers from military service. Underground Railroad crossing points at Ironton.
Portsmouth served as a wartime logistics point. Iron and salt production for the war effort.
Iron production for war effort. Salt production continued.
Remote and lightly populated. Men enlisted but the county saw no military action.

key_infrastructure.name: Battle of Buffington Island
key_infrastructure.impact: Morgan's Raiders were intercepted crossing the Ohio River in Meigs County. The battle was the only significant Civil War engagement fought in the cluster. Confederate prisoners were captured; Morgan escaped northward before being captured in Columbiana County.

key_infrastructure.name: Hanging Rock iron furnaces — wartime production
key_infrastructure.impact: Iron production for the Union was strategically important. Furnace workers were sometimes exempted from military service. Production ran at or near capacity throughout the war.

key_infrastructure.name: Underground Railroad crossing points
key_infrastructure.impact: Multiple crossing points on the Ohio River — Ironton, Gallipolis, Marietta, Pomeroy. Freedom seekers crossed from Virginia to Ohio with help from conductors on both sides of the river. The river was the most heavily crossed section of the Underground Railroad in Ohio.

major_employers.name: Union Army
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of men from the eight-county region served.

major_employers.name: Hanging Rock iron furnaces (wartime production)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds — some workers exempted from military service due to strategic importance.

wage_benchmarks.source: Harper, Robert S. Ohio in the Civil War. Columbus: Ohio Historical Society, 1961.
Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945).

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The war was across the river — {ancestor_name} could see Virginia from the bluff, hear the rumors of raiders and guerrillas, and watch the gunboats pass on the Ohio. When Morgan's cavalry crossed into Meigs County in July 1863, the war was not across the river anymore — it was in the cornfield.
economic_eras.source: Harper, Robert S. Ohio in the Civil War. Columbus: Ohio Historical Society, 1961. Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945).

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Charcoal iron industry collapses (replaced by coke-fired operations); coal mining expands in Hocking Valley and Sunday Creek; oil and gas boom; hill-farm subdivision; beginning of out-migration
economic_eras.description: The postwar decades brought the most dramatic economic transformation in the cluster's history. The Hanging Rock charcoal iron industry — the region's largest employer — collapsed between the 1870s and 1890s as coke-fired furnaces using Pittsburgh-area coal produced cheaper iron. Over seventy furnaces went cold, and the communities built around them emptied. In their place came coal: the Hocking Valley and Sunday Creek coal fields in Athens, Meigs, and Vinton counties drew a new workforce, including immigrant miners from Wales, Ireland, and Southern and Eastern Europe. Oil and gas were discovered in Washington County and elsewhere in the 1860s, creating a brief boom. But the fundamental problem remained: the hill-country farms were subdividing past viability as each generation divided land that had never been productive enough. The most enterprising young people were already leaving — for Columbus, Dayton, Cleveland, and the industrial cities of the Midwest.

county_specifics.note: Oil and gas boom brought temporary prosperity. Petroleum discovered in the 1860s. Marietta continued as institutional center but fell behind growing industrial cities.
Sunday Creek coal field developed. Mining towns (Chauncey, Glouster, Murray City) appeared. Ohio University grew slowly. Two economies: college town and coal hollows.
Coal mining expanded. Pomeroy remained a river town. Some oil and gas production.
No major industrial replacement for the failed subsistence economy. Population decline began.
Iron furnaces went cold. Ironton's ironmaster mansions became relics. Some coal mining. Population began to decline.
Canal era ended. Portsmouth reinvented itself — shoe manufacturing replaced iron and salt. The Selby Shoe Company and competitors employed thousands.
Iron furnaces closed. Salt production declined. Welsh community maintained cultural institutions but the economic base was gone.
Some coal mining. Most charcoal iron furnaces cold. Population peaked and began to decline.

key_infrastructure.name: Collapse of charcoal iron industry
key_infrastructure.impact: Coke-fired furnaces in Pittsburgh and elsewhere produced cheaper iron, making charcoal iron obsolete. Over seventy Hanging Rock furnaces closed. Entire communities collapsed.

key_infrastructure.name: Hocking Valley / Sunday Creek coal development
key_infrastructure.impact: Coal mining replaced iron as the primary extractive industry. Mining towns appeared in Athens, Meigs, Vinton, and Jackson counties. The mines drew immigrant workers.

key_infrastructure.name: Oil and gas discovery
key_infrastructure.impact: Petroleum discovered in Washington County and elsewhere. Brief boom. Derricks appeared on ridge tops. Most operations exhausted within decades.

key_infrastructure.name: Portsmouth shoe industry
key_infrastructure.impact: Shoe manufacturing replaced iron as Portsmouth's primary industry. The Selby Shoe Company was the largest employer, at peak employing over 2,000 workers.

major_employers.name: Coal mining companies (multiple)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands across the region. Individual mines employed 50-200 workers.

major_employers.name: Shoe factories (Portsmouth)
major_employers.employment_scale: 2,000+ at peak. Portsmouth became the 'shoe capital of Ohio.'

wage_benchmarks.source: Eller, Ronald D. Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1982, ch. 6.
Federal Writers' Project. The Ohio Guide (1940).
Stout, Geology of Water in Ohio (1927).
Eller, Uneven Ground (2008), ch. 1.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The furnaces went cold, one by one — each dark stack another piece of the economy dying. {ancestor_name} had a choice: follow the work to the coal mines, follow the Scioto north to Columbus, or stay on a hill farm that had never been good enough and was getting worse with every generation that divided it.
economic_eras.source: Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945). Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers (1982). Federal Writers' Project, The Ohio Guide (1940).

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Coal mining mechanized and declining; mine closures; hill-farm abandonment; Great Depression devastation; out-migration accelerates to Dayton, Columbus, Cleveland, Detroit
economic_eras.description: Between 1910 and 1940, southeastern Ohio became the Appalachian poverty region that would draw national attention in the 1960s. Coal mining mechanized — cutting machines and loading machines replaced pick miners — and the workforce shrank even as output held steady. Mine closures in the Sunday Creek and Hocking Valley districts threw thousands out of work. Hill farms that had been marginal for generations were abandoned as families left for factory cities. The Great Depression struck a region already in economic crisis: unemployment in some townships exceeded fifty percent. The WPA, CCC, and other New Deal programs provided the first significant government employment. Wayne National Forest (established 1934) began acquiring abandoned farmland for reforestation. The 1937 Ohio River flood devastated Pomeroy and Portsmouth. Out-migration accelerated — young people left for Dayton, Columbus, Cleveland, and Detroit in a pattern identical to Appalachian out-migration from West Virginia and Kentucky.

county_specifics.note: Oil production declined. Marietta held together as a county seat but the surrounding hill country emptied. CCC camps in the county.
Sunday Creek coal mines closed or mechanized. Mining towns like Murray City became ghost towns. Ohio University was the county's economic anchor.
1937 flood devastated Pomeroy. Coal mining declined. Population loss accelerated.
Deep poverty. No significant employer. WPA and CCC provided the only non-farm employment.
Iron industry was a memory. Some coal mining. Ironton declined. Population loss.
Shoe factories maintained employment through the Depression. The 1937 flood devastated Portsmouth. Floodwall construction afterward.
Iron and salt were dead. Coal declining. The Welsh community aged. Population loss.
The most depressed county in Ohio. No significant employer. Wayne National Forest acquired large tracts of abandoned farmland.

key_infrastructure.name: 1937 Ohio River flood
key_infrastructure.impact: The worst flood in recorded Ohio River history. Pomeroy and Portsmouth devastated. Thousands evacuated. Record damage at multiple courthouses. Floodwall construction followed in Portsmouth and other river towns.

key_infrastructure.name: Wayne National Forest established
key_infrastructure.impact: The federal government began acquiring abandoned farmland for reforestation. CCC camps planted trees and built trails. The forest eventually covered over 240,000 acres across southeastern Ohio.

key_infrastructure.name: New Straitsville mine fire
key_infrastructure.impact: A mine fire set during an 1884 strike continued burning underground for decades — one of the most persistent mine fires in American history. The fire symbolized the violence and desperation of the coal economy.

major_employers.name: WPA / CCC / other New Deal programs
major_employers.employment_scale: Multiple CCC camps and WPA projects across the region. For many families, the first steady paycheck in years.

major_employers.name: Shoe factories (Portsmouth, declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining from peak but still 1,000+ workers.

major_employers.name: Coal mines (mechanizing, declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining — mechanization reduced the workforce even as some mines continued operating.

major_employers.name: Ohio University
major_employers.employment_scale: Growing slowly — the only institutional employer of consequence in the county.

wage_benchmarks.source: Federal Writers' Project. The Ohio Guide (1940).
Eller, Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers, epilogue.
Eller, Uneven Ground (2008), ch. 1.
Federal Writers' Project. The Ohio Guide (1940).

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, southeastern Ohio was what the newspapers would later call Appalachian poverty — but {ancestor_name}'s family did not need a newspaper to tell them what they already knew. The mines were closing, the farms were failing, and the young people who left for Dayton or Columbus were not coming back.
economic_eras.source: Eller, Uneven Ground (2008). Federal Writers' Project, The Ohio Guide (1940).

economic_eras.era_name: War, GI Bill, and the Great Out-Migration
economic_eras.dominant_economy: WWII military service takes young men out; some wartime industrial employment; GI Bill sends veterans to college or trade school; massive out-migration to Dayton, Columbus, Cleveland, Detroit; poverty designation; institutional employers (Ohio University) become dominant
economic_eras.description: World War II broke the remaining hold of southeastern Ohio on its young men. Military service took thousands out of the hills — many for the first time in their lives. They saw cities, factories, and opportunities that the hollows had never offered. The GI Bill gave returning veterans access to college and trade schools, and many used it to leave permanently. The out-migration that had been building since 1900 became a flood in the 1940s and 1950s. Appalachian Ohioans joined Appalachian West Virginians and Kentuckians in the factory cities of the Midwest — Dayton, Columbus, Cleveland, Detroit, Akron. The migration was kinship-driven: one family member went first, found work, and sent word back. Within five years, extended families had reassembled in working-class neighborhoods of destination cities. Those who remained in southeastern Ohio faced a shrinking economy. Ohio University in Athens expanded after the war and became the dominant employer in the county. Elsewhere, there was little: some coal mining, some light manufacturing, government employment, and subsistence farming that was no longer viable but continued because the families had nothing else.

county_specifics.note: Marietta maintained a modest industrial base. Some manufacturing. But population continued to decline in the hill country.
Ohio University expanded dramatically with GI Bill enrollment. The university became the county's dominant employer and economic engine. The Sunday Creek coal hollows continued to empty.
Pomeroy rebuilt after the 1937 flood. Population declined. No significant new employers.
Deep poverty continued. The Bob Evans farm restaurant chain was founded in Gallia County (1946) — a small bright spot.
Ironton declined. Some river-related industry. The Appalachian migration to northern Ohio cities accelerated.
Portsmouth shoe industry declined. Floodwall construction (1938-1948) provided temporary employment. The city contracted.
Small manufacturing. Welsh cultural institutions faded as the language-speaking generation aged. Population loss.
The most rural and most impoverished county in Ohio. Wayne National Forest continued to acquire land. The county's population was lower than at any time since its formation.

key_infrastructure.name: GI Bill
key_infrastructure.impact: Gave southeastern Ohio veterans access to education and training. Many used it to attend Ohio University, other state schools, or trade programs — and then left the region permanently. The GI Bill was the single most effective out-migration mechanism in the cluster's history.

key_infrastructure.name: Portsmouth floodwall completion
key_infrastructure.impact: The concrete floodwall protected Portsmouth from future flooding but also severed the city from its riverfront. The floodwall later became a canvas for civic murals depicting the city's history.

key_infrastructure.name: Ohio University postwar expansion
key_infrastructure.impact: GI Bill enrollment swelled the university. By 1960, Ohio University was the largest employer in Athens County and one of the largest in the region.

major_employers.name: Ohio University
major_employers.employment_scale: Growing from hundreds to over 1,000 employees by 1960.

major_employers.name: Coal mines (continued decline)
major_employers.employment_scale: Shrinking. Mechanization continued to reduce workforce.

major_employers.name: U.S. military service
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of men from the region served.

wage_benchmarks.source: Ohio University personnel records.
Eller, Uneven Ground (2008), ch. 3.
Eller, Uneven Ground (2008), ch. 4.
Eller, Uneven Ground (2008), ch. 1.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The war took {ancestor_name}'s sons out of the hollows and showed them cities where a man could earn in a week what the farm produced in a month. The GI Bill gave them a ticket that did not require going back. By the {decade}s, the family reunion was in Dayton or Columbus, not on the home place.
economic_eras.source: Eller, Ronald D. Uneven Ground: Appalachia since 1945. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2008.

economic_turning_points.event: Ohio Company settlement at Marietta — first organized settlement in the Northwest Territory
economic_turning_points.impact: Established the institutional framework for the region: courts, property records, and the precedent of organized settlement. The Northwest Ordinance's prohibition of slavery north of the Ohio River made the region fundamentally different from Virginia and Kentucky across the river.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: Marietta was not just the first town in Ohio — it was the first place in the Northwest Territory where free soil had a courthouse and a recorder. {ancestor_name}'s deed was recorded in a system that began with the Ohio Company.
economic_turning_points.source: Hildreth, Pioneer History (1848). Cayton, The Frontier Republic (1986).

economic_turning_points.event: Ohio & Erie Canal reaches Portsmouth — southeastern Ohio connected to the interior
economic_turning_points.impact: The canal connected the Ohio River to Columbus and Cleveland, making Portsmouth a commercial junction. Goods could now move by water from the Gulf of Mexico to Lake Erie through southeastern Ohio.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The canal turned Portsmouth from a river landing into a crossroads — {ancestor_name} could ship pork downstream to New Orleans or north to Cleveland, and the choice defined which direction the family's future went.

economic_turning_points.event: Morgan's Raid crosses the Ohio River — war comes to Ohio soil
economic_turning_points.impact: Confederate cavalry under John Hunt Morgan crossed the Ohio River into Meigs County in July 1863. The Battle of Buffington Island was the only significant Civil War engagement in the cluster. The raid terrified the civilian population and demonstrated that the river was not an impassable barrier.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When Morgan's raiders crossed the Ohio into Meigs County, the war stopped being something that happened on the other side of the river. {ancestor_name} saw Confederate cavalry in Ohio — the only time the war came to free soil in this part of the state.
economic_turning_points.source: Harper, Ohio in the Civil War (1961).

economic_turning_points.event: Collapse of the charcoal iron industry
economic_turning_points.impact: Coke-fired furnaces made charcoal iron obsolete. Over seventy Hanging Rock furnaces closed within two decades. The largest industrial employer in the region vanished, and the communities built around the furnaces emptied.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When the last Hanging Rock furnace went cold, it took more than an industry — it took the only source of cash wages most families in Lawrence and Jackson counties had ever known.
economic_turning_points.source: Stout and Schoenlaub, Iron Ore Resources (1945).

economic_turning_points.event: Ohio River flood devastates Portsmouth and Pomeroy
economic_turning_points.impact: The worst flood in recorded history on the Ohio River. Portsmouth under twenty feet of water; Pomeroy under ten. Thousands evacuated. Some courthouse records damaged. Floodwall construction followed.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The 1937 flood put Portsmouth under twenty feet of water and Pomeroy under ten — the river that had built these towns came close to destroying them, and the floodwalls that followed walled the towns off from the water forever.

narrative_hooks: Southeastern Ohio was settled by two peoples — New England veterans at Marietta with their institutions, and Virginia-Kentucky hill farmers in the backcountry with their corn patches. {ancestor_name}'s world depended on which stream {pronoun_subject} descended from.
The Hanging Rock furnaces lit the night sky of Lawrence and Jackson counties for fifty years — and when they went dark, the region went dark with them.

The Ohio River was the boundary between slave and free — {ancestor_name} lived on the free side, but the south bank of Kentucky was visible on any clear day, close enough to remind everyone that freedom was geographic.
Coal replaced iron as the thing the hills were good for — but coal camps paid in scrip, not cash, and the company store took back what the mine had given.
By the {decade}s, southeastern Ohio's primary export was people — young families heading up Route 23 to Columbus or west on Route 35 to Dayton, following the kin who had gone before them.
The GI Bill was southeastern Ohio's great liberation and great loss — it gave {ancestor_name}'s children a way out of the hollows and a reason never to come back.
Ohio University sat in the hills like a lighthouse in a shipwreck — the one institution in southeastern Ohio that grew while everything around it shrank.
source_references: Hildreth, Samuel P. Pioneer History: Being an Account of the First Examinations of the Ohio Valley. Cincinnati: H. W. Derby, 1848.
Cayton, Andrew R. L. The Frontier Republic: Ideology and Politics in the Ohio Country, 1780-1825. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1986.
Stout, Wilber, and R. A. Schoenlaub. The Iron Ore Resources of Ohio. Ohio Geological Survey Bulletin 45. Columbus, 1945.
Scheiber, Harry N. Ohio Canal Era: A Case Study of Government and the Economy, 1820-1861. Athens: Ohio University Press, 1969.
Eller, Ronald D. Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Eller, Ronald D. Uneven Ground: Appalachia since 1945. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 2008.
Harper, Robert S. Ohio in the Civil War. Columbus: Ohio Historical Society, 1961.
Jakle, John A. 'Salt and the Initial Settlement of the Ohio Valley.' Ph.D. diss., Indiana University, 1967.
Federal Writers' Project. The Ohio Guide. New York: Oxford University Press, 1940.
Trotter, Joe William. River Jordan: African American Urban Life in the Ohio Valley. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1998.
